chapters.title: Writing mastery is the art of working with reality
chapters.text:
The writer's craft consists of many features from the author's side, including creating a plot and conflict, developing characters, building the book structure, and balancing objective reality, like other authors evaluating their colleagues during workshops. However, craft does not exist separately from modern realities, even if the author describes a fantasy world inhabited by elves or aliens. Real-world context and a writer's work with this context is a craft.

When we talk about the craft of writing, the first to come to mind are the standards laid down in ancient Greece. The famous philosopher Aristotle believed that the hero should be the primary driver of the plot. However, the craft is more than the actions of one hero.

Writing is a set of expectations shaped by readers and books created earlier.

A privileged audience (predominantly white heterosexual men) most often shapes expectations. Matthew Salesses believes that writers should rethink the craft for a variety of modern stories since the human experience is multifaceted, and they should fully reveal it in their work. An unspoken rule of writing (which Salesses opposes) states that a reader will automatically consider a character’s race white if the writer does not reference it. But if the mention of race affects the story, then, like the setting, race should be part of any craft. Salesses explores the inclusiveness of works; in any case, it will be correct to indicate the race of each character, not just people of color.

Aristotle, whose principles many white male writers try to follow, believed that as long as slavery existed, masters could create. Society considered writing a privilege; only educated, successful, and wealthy people could write for publications. But in real life, people do not choose their class and race, so it is important to bring diverse characters onto the stage. The world is changing, and stories should inevitably change with it.

The world of creativity does not exist without a real context. Craft in the real world will help you create for an audience whose expectations are essential to you as an author. Matthew Salesses offers writers techniques that will liberate their work, make it more natural and valuable for both readers and themselves. The author also provides new ideas for conducting writing workshops, which, in his opinion, should focus more on the author's personality and background. Also, in summary, you will find some valuable exercises for writing a book.

chapters.title: Culture and expectations influence the craft
chapters.text:
Have you ever noticed that we consume the entire text and do not grasp each word separately? Salesses teaches his students to use "say" and "asked" instead of distracting words like "queried." The fact is that we do not focus on the word "asked," unlike "queried" — we just read the sentence in the appropriate interrogative intonation; we are used to seeing "say" and "asked" in most books. But if all authors began to write "queried," this word would also become invisible. It is the influence of shared culture; writers will not use words that are not common among their readers. The craft is understanding how to use cultural expectations to your advantage. For example, writers who are not native speakers try to use many synonyms in their first works in English. However, even Hemingway used repetitive dialogue tags because they were familiar to the readers. You can repeat words without using complex vocabulary unless your audience requires it.

There is a vast number of cultures and traditions with different meanings. Imagine writers from a cultural environment where everyone says "query." They come to a workshop where all the authors use "ask." Those authors would advise the "query" writers to replace the unknown word, and as a result, the book’s audience completely changes.

If writers really believe that art is important to actual life, then the responsibilities of actual life are the responsibilities of art. ~ Matthew Salesses

Matthew Salesses

How does Matthew Salesses define craft? Since craft is a set of expectations, it has standards. When we read a book, we have certain cultural expectations. For example, Western psychological realism describes a person becoming a part of society. For instance, Elizabeth Bennet meets and marries Mr. Darcy, and Daisy Buchanan from The Great Gatsby returns to her husband, leaving a fleeting infatuation behind. They did what society expected of them, and it doesn't matter if they were happy in the end. Thus, social norms capture the heroes and the novel. But expectations are not always bad things. Some archetypes work well, for example, in romantic comedies — the writer Kurt Vonnegut joked that a story like Cinderella immediately brings the author a million dollars.

The way we tell stories influences how we interpret meaning in our daily life.

In the book The Hero with a Thousand Faces, Joseph Campbell introduces monomyth theory, where the main male character takes the same path with the same trials from story to story. In fact, his idea is very selective. Why can't the heroine's journey be at the center of the plot? The story can also be about heroes who do not go through trials.

chapters.title: Writers create for the whole world, but they always have a specific audience
chapters.text:
Using a craft means interacting with an audience. Stories are always meant for someone, and by default, the writer tries to play to the privileged audience.

The plot follows the standard scheme in the generally accepted tradition: exposure, provoking a conflict, action, culmination, conflict resolution, finale. This form of a story is also a reflection of a specific cultural tradition. A four-act structure called kishotenketsu — introduction, development, twist, reconciliation — dominates Asian tradition. Western critics describe the Chinese literary tradition as formless, but Chinese critic Zheng Zhenduo describes it as structurally sound. The truth is it is just designed for a specific audience.

Craft is inseparable from identity. Craft does not exist outside of society, outside of culture, outside of power. ~ Matthew Salesses

Matthew Salesses

The goal of a writer is to think about the audience that will understand their idea from the first word; they don’t have to worry about satisfying an audience that doesn't belong to them. At the same time, Salesses does not claim that white people cannot read African fiction. Still, it will take more time and effort for them to understand this literature. In the case of Thousand and One Nights translation, translators often cut out repetitions to adapt the text to a specific audience. However, in another culture, this repetitive technique is considered a mastery.

The concept of "good writing" depends on who the reader is.

Sometimes African Americans write for two audiences at once: white people because it will help their career, and Black people because they will understand them. Authors encrypt hint keys for their audience in a technique called hidden narrative. Understanding the culture and your audience helps you craft your writing with greater precision and relevance.

Salesses also covers the concepts of implied reader and author. The implied reader perceives the heroes of the story as if they were real people. An implied author is the persona that readers perceive as the storyteller through the narrative. For example, imagine two different descriptions of the 9/11 tragedy. One will focus on inner experiences and feelings, and the second will be more detailed, describing the events around the tragedy. So, the implied author and reader from the first description are both Americans old enough to remember the day of the tragedy. The implied author and reader from the second may be foreigners who need explanations. The writer should think about the purpose (the combination of topic and audience) of their writing.

chapters.title: Tone creates an attitude towards the world, and conflict reflects reality
chapters.text:
Matthew Salesses shares practical terms about the craft. The tone is an attitude towards the world, and the writer Robert Boswell defines it as the distance between narrator and character. For example, comic novels can have serious characters. Thus, readers will perceive the gloomy hero written in a humorous tone as funny (even if the character takes themselves seriously).

The tone also shows the attitude of the characters towards each other and towards the world. Tone indicates how the reader should relate to the characters. Remember the difference between emotion and tone. The tone lasts throughout the story, but a book that focuses on just one emotion would be weird. Emotions must vary. In a sorrowful story, characters might feel anger or joy.

At the same time, the tone does not reflect the attitude of the protagonist to the world. For example, imagine the coming-of-age story. It can start with a country boy who comes to conquer a big city where people are different; dreams are in no hurry to come true. In this story, the meaning depends not only on the character but also on the city itself. There are several options for the development of events: the guy learns to love the city as it is; the city changes as a result of the guy's efforts or any other. The tone of the story will vary with the character because the world around him will change.

The next tool is conflict. Fiction does not exist in a vacuum. The author makes a choice that will become part of the conflict in his story, including racial, sociocultural, and class contexts. Most often, the conflict concerns the protagonist, who also solves it. But conflict is not just a thrilling element; it is a worldview. Choosing between the regular motives "man vs. man," "man vs. the world," or "man vs. himself" means selecting a particular model of the world, which means identifying the audience and the message for it.

If you want to make a conflict powerful, make it seem real.

Character arc and story arc work together. The character arc involves changes, or a lack of change, and the story arc concerns the world around the protagonist. For example, consider a story about a poor and honest character who becomes rich and immoral. In this case, the character arc is a moral fall, and the plot arc is a "rags to riches" motif. A character who receives power and money by giving up principles indicates that nothing can change the world. A Cinderella story has the same motive — society requires a girl to get married to fit in. As you create the story and characters, remember that they work together but show different morals.

chapters.title: The character should be unique, not relatable
chapters.text:
The element of characterization means "revitalizing" characters, bringing them into reality. One of the methods of working out a character is to develop their actions, appearances, and thoughts. But in fact, what defines a character is their attitude toward other characters.

Credibility is the ability for the author to control and use the story to say something about the world of the story and the world where we live. The setting is what the reader notices. Try answering the following questions as you create your setting: What shapes your character? What do they know? What shapes the narrator, and what do they know?

The structure of a work is its organization. It depends on the order of events. Salesses recalls the story of the writer at one of the workshops. It was a story about a Black woman whose brother was a drug dealer and a white police officer next door who wanted to do good deeds. The author has organized the story to start and end with the point of view of the person of color. A story with the same events but from a white police officer's point of view would have a different meaning. At the same time, in some cultures, the structure is cyclical — and this is normal since it has a special meaning.

Comparing yourself to a character from a book while reading is inevitable.

Relatability does not apply to craft. Readers' expectations of fiction are related to their previous experience of fiction, which is culture. A character's hobbies should characterize them, not make them relevant. When an author makes relatability part of their craft, they begin to endow a character (like an alien) with characteristics that the audience (mostly white) will understand. Making a piece relatable on purpose is really about making a statement about what your audience and their expectations should be. Write for people, but don't decide for them.

Did you know? In America, the "invisible" style of literature is widespread. It is the desire to abandon metaphors, the rejection of abstractions, and the desire to avoid open politics. Instead of a political argument, the character can angrily eat a potato. Ernest Hemingway, and later Raymond Carver, popularized this style.

chapters.title: Ideas for new workshops and exercises for writers
chapters.text:
Matthew Salesses's workshops model offers to work with stories that will help the author see what they had done and what else they can do with the material. The workshop participants should know what expectations the author is working with, and what he wants to implement. A helpful workshop looks at each audience separately and approaches stories from a personal perspective.

One author's treasure may be useless to another.

Salesses recalls a workshop he conducted for authors solely using questions; he wanted to shift the focus of the workshop to the personality of the author. The authors are usually silent during the sessions and only listen to the comments of others. But writers should speak up, defend their opinions and explain their positions. Otherwise, their story risks losing its purpose and audience.

Workshop options that Salesses believes will be useful for writers:

1. Critical Response Process: Liz Lerman proposed this model that consists of the following stages: observation (the author listens to the reaction of others to their work), questions from the author to those present, questions from the workshop participants to the author, and, if the writer allows, participants’ suggestions for their work.
2. Workshops of questions and suggestions: During the entire workshop, either the author or the participants ask questions. The same with suggestive workshops — only the author or other writers share suggestions for improving the novel.
3. Everyone Workshops at the Same Time: Suitable for discussion of short stories or drafts. All workshop participants read their work and discuss it.
4. Debate: The author submits a list of craft solutions with their manuscript and divides participants into two teams, where one defends the author's solutions, and the other criticizes them.

Here are some exercises for writers:

• Work with the story's tone by writing a scene with a sad character in a cheerful tone (or vice versa). After writing the story, analyze how you created the world and the character: the setting, the person's characteristics, and so on.
• Read the piece aloud to yourself or a friend. Ask what seems dull in the story. Reading aloud, you will hear what sounds out of tune.
• Get rid of one or more characters. How does deleting them change the story?
• Review your story and highlight abstract moments, such as ideas or emotions. Try replacing them with more specific expressions.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Matthew Salesses's new craft and workshop model proposes an extension of existing models in writers' craft. A character is more than a set of traits that the reader recognizes in themselves (or wants to recognize). The story can be uncomfortable, but it should be honest. Let the world test your protagonist. Let the hero become a real person, with their voice and thoughts.

Always keep your audience in mind when writing a story. Don't try to please everyone; your audience will be grateful for the story you tell. Expand the scope of your craft by addressing the diversity of people, characters, and destinies when creating the world of your story. Remember that conflict does not mean that the hero should overcome obstacles on the way to themselves; it can be broader and more profound. The cultural context influences the craft. Free your story from stereotypes. Answer the questions: Who is your audience? What is this story about? What does the main character want, and will they change in the end? Will the world around us change? Let your craft become a tool for the truthful portraying of reality.

According to Salesses, workshops should be an act of shared imagination. Writers must confront the dangers of workshops to text, such as depersonalization and cultural erasure, and workshops should become a place of mutual help. Writers love the feeling of power over the texts of others, but it’s wrong. Authors can be more successful if they share experiences and help each other.

Try this

These two exercises can help shape your story:

• Read the first page of the story to your friends. Let them describe what they heard and offer their version of the events. Write the first page again and compare them.
• Exercise by Danzy Senna: In five minutes, write as many features of your character as possible using the formula "She/he/they were the type of person who…". Make these characteristics extraordinary.

Read it till the end?
